title: Deleting Duplicates in a Sorted Linked List

statement.markdown: You are given a sorted linked list and you need to delete all duplicate elements. The linked list is head node, and each node contains an integer data and a pointer to the next node. For example, a linked list with values 1 -> 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 3 -> 3 should become 1 -> 2 -> 3.
statement.inputFormat: The input is a linked list head node and you need to return the updated head node.
statement.outputFormat: The output is the updated head node of the linked list.
statement.notes: You need to return the updated head node. The head may be changed after you delete the duplicates. The time complexity is expected to be O(n) where n is the number of nodes in the linked list.

hints.text: Use a set to keep track of the elements you have seen so far.

editorial.title: Correcting the Set Usage in the Linked List
editorial.intuition: Using a set to keep track of the elements you have seen so far will ensure you do not add duplicates to the linked list.
editorial.bruteForce: Iterate through the linked list and check if the current element is in the set for duplicates. If it is, skip it. Otherwise, add it to the set and the linked list.
editorial.betterSolution: Iterate through the linked list and add each element to a set. If the set is empty or the current element is not equal to the last element in the set, add it to the set and the linked list.
editorial.optimalSolution: Iterate through the linked list and add each element to a set. If the set is empty or the current element is not equal to the last element in the set, add it to the set and the linked list.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The optimal solution works because it uses a set to keep track of the elements you have seen so far. This ensures you do not add duplicates to the linked list.
editorial.dryRun: Iterate through the linked list and add each element to a set. If the set is empty or the current element is not equal to the last element in the set, add it to the set and the linked list.
commonMistakes: Using an array to keep track of the elements you have seen so far.
Not checking if the current element is not equal to the last element in the set.

solutions.python:
class Solution:
    def deleteDuplicates(self, head: ListNode) -> ListNode:
        dummy = ListNode(0)
        dummy.next = head
        current = dummy
        seen = set()
        while current.next:
            if current.next.val not in seen:
                seen.add(current.next.val)
                current = current.next
            else:
                current.next = current.next.next
        return dummy.next

print('-----')
head = ListNode(1)
head.next = ListNode(1)
head.next.next = ListNode(2)
head.next.next.next = ListNode(3)
head.next.next.next.next = ListNode(3)
head.next.next.next.next.next = ListNode(3)
solution = Solution()
dummy = solution.deleteDuplicates(head)
while dummy:
    print(dummy.val, end=' -> ')
    dummy = dummy.next
print('None')
